coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of orientationally grown CQD/TiO2 thin film on FTO substrate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Shilpa, G.
P, Mohan Kumar
Deepthi, P.R.
Balan, Ramdas
Sukhdev, Anu
Bhaskar, Pradeep
Kumar D, Kishore
coredata.dc:description: We have studied the enhanced light harvesting capacity and reduced recombination rate of TiO2 thin film by composite with Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). The CQD/TiO2 film was synthesized using facile, and low-temperature hydrothermal synthesis approach using CQD derived from sugarcane juice and ethanol. The prepared TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 composite thin films were characterized for structural, functional, optical, morphological and elemental compositions analysis using, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman Spectroscopy, UV–Vis Spectroscopy, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The optical band gap of CQD/TiO2 was calculated to be 2.87 eV fall under the visible region favorable for photocatalytic activity under sunlight. The Methylene Blue (MB) dye degradation studies of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films were carried out under the noon sunlight radiation which showed 78% and 82.6% degradation with good reusability. Therefore, this CQD/TiO2 composite thin film can be a promising candidate for industrial dye effluent treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Hydrothermal method
CQD/TiO2
Reusability
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQD/TiO2 thin film
Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) studies
Plausible mechanism and reusability of photo catalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2 and (b) CQD/TiO2.
SSP plot of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2 and (b) CQD/TiO2.
FTIR spectra of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2 and (b) CQD/TiO2.
Raman spectra of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2 and (b) CQD/TiO2.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of thin film coated on FTO plate (a) CQDs.
Tauc plot of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2 and (b) CQD/TiO2.
FESEM images of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2: top-view, (b) TiO2: cross-sectional view and (c) CQD/TiO2: top-view & (d) CQD/TiO2: cross-sectional view.
EDX spectra of thin films coated on FTO plate (a) TiO2 and (b) CQD/TiO2.
XPS spectra of CQD/TiO2 coated on FTO glass plate (a) Survey spectrum (b) Ti 2p, (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s.

Photocatalytic degradation studies of MB (a) UV–visible spectra of MB dye at different catalytic time using TiO2 and (b) UV–visible spectra of MB dye at different catalytic time using CQD/TiO2, (c) C\C0 vs time plot and (d) –ln(C\C0) vs time plot.
(a) Energy band scheme for CQD/TiO2 thin film, (b) Reusability of CQD/TiO2 thin film for the degradation of MB dye.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of orientationally grown CQD/TiO2 thin film on FTO substrate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Hradec Králové, Rokitanského 62, 500 03, Hradec Králové, Czech Republic
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, University of Hradec Kralove, Rokitanskeho 62, 500 03, Hradec Kralove, Czech Republic

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Physics, School of Engineering, Presidency University, Bangalore, 560064, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have studied the enhanced light harvesting capacity and reduced recombination rate of TiO2 thin film by composite with Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). The CQD/TiO2 film was synthesized using facile, and low-temperature hydrothermal synthesis approach using CQD derived from sugarcane juice and ethanol. The prepared TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 composite thin films were characterized for structural, functional, optical, morphological and elemental compositions analysis using, X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman Spectroscopy, UV–Vis Spectroscopy, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The optical band gap of CQD/TiO2 was calculated to be 2.87 eV fall under the visible region favorable for photocatalytic activity under sunlight. The Methylene Blue (MB) dye degradation studies of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films were carried out under the noon sunlight radiation which showed 78% and 82.6% degradation with good reusability. Therefore, this CQD/TiO2 composite thin film can be a promising candidate for industrial dye effluent treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern times, water pollution has become a huge threat and it is found to be increasing due to the advancement of technology and industrialization to meet the needs of the rapid population. Consequently, industrial organic effluents like drugs, dyes, pesticides, etc. are commonly discovered in water sources and those have long-term consequences on the environment [1]. These toxic chemicals from various industrial settings needs an appropriate waste management treatments in order to minimize the contaminants in water [2].

Multiple strategies for removing organic pollutants and various hazardous ions from polluted water have been developed like membrane filtration, chemical coagulation, biological degradation techniques and Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) [3]. AOPs based photocatalysis using TiO2 photocatalyst for water treatment is acknowledged and commercialized [4]. However, due to its wide band gap of energy (3.0 eV–3.2 eV) it is suitable to oxidize pollutants efficiently only under UV light irradiation. To overcome its disadvantages, composites with carbon is a favorable approach that can produce electron-hole pairs through visible light absorption [5]. Using carbon has advantages, it can be prepared via eco-friendly method, and it exists in different forms [6–10]. Numerous carbon materials composite with TiO2 and various carbon forms have been researched and demonstrated. Nevertheless, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are gradually becoming promising carbon nanomaterials due to their unique properties [11–13]. It also functioned as electron reservoirs [14–16]. Inadequate interfacial adhesion between CQDs and parent catalysts may not support electron transfer. In such case, the in -situ hydrothermal synthesis can be considered where CQDs can be chemically attached to the TiO2 surfaces via –OH groups on their surface [17].

Thick or thin film-coated photocatalysts are advantageous over the particulate because of no secondary contamination of water during the treatment. Hence, the catalyst can be reused with minimum or no change in the degradation efficiency [18–21]. There are few reports on carbon-TiO2 thin films that are studied for an efficient photocatalytic dye degradation process [22–24]. In the present work, we have investigated the photocatalytic performance of orientationally grown rutile – TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films on the FTO glass plate by in-situ hydrothermal method. The results were compared with the rutile- TiO2 thin films to envisage the role of CQD with TiO2 composite for improved photodegradation of methylene blue dye under direct sunlight.

Titanium tetra isopropoxide (TTIP) (Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt Ltd) with 97% purity was used as Ti precursor. Sugarcane juice procured from the local market was used as a source for the preparation of CQDs. FTO glass plates (Technistro, India) of 3.0 cm × 3.0 cm, with thickness of 1.1 mm and resistivity ≤10 Ω/sq. Deionized water (DI), HCl with 36% purity (Nice chemicals Pvt Ltd) and ethanol (Nice chemicals Pvt Ltd) were used. Throughout the trials, all chemicals and DI water were used without additional filtration.

A one-step hydrothermal method was employed to prepare TiO2 thin film on the FTO glass substrate in an acidic condition employing a dilute HCl solution [25]. Dilute HCl stock solution was prepared by mixing HCl and deionized water in a ratio of 1:2. In the typical procedure, 0.2 ml TTIP was added to 30 ml of dilute HCl stock solution and stirred for 30 min until a clear and transparent solution was obtained which is called precursor solution. The cleaned FTO glass using ethanol and water medium in the ultasonicator bath was placed inclined on to the wall of the Teflon beaker and followed, precursor solution was filled. The sealed Teflon beaker in stainless autoclave was kept in a hot air oven (Presto, India) and heated for 20 h at 180 °C. The coated FTO glass plate was removed from the autoclave when it had naturally cooled to room temperature, cleaned with DI water, and annealed at 300 °C for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and CQD/TiO2 thin film
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs was synthesized by a green synthesis route from the source of sugarcane juice by hydrothermal method [26]. The process involved filtration of raw sugarcane to remove the solid particles, from the filtered juice of 56 ml and 24 ml of ethanol were mixed for 45 min with the aid of a magnetic stirrer. The mixture was then transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon, sealed, and heated in a hot air oven for 3 h at 120 °C. The yielded brownish-yellow solution was washed with dichloromethane to remove the unreacted particles and further washed with acetone by centrifugation at 4500 rpm. The upper light brown solution was collected and characterized using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Systronics Type 2202).

A similar procedure of TiO2 coating was followed for CQD/TiO2 coating on the FTO coated glass plate by hydrothermal method, with an additional procedure of CQDs solution being added to the transparent precursor and stirred for 15 min. The sealed Teflon beaker containing FTO plate and precursor solution in a stainless autoclave was kept in a hot air oven and heated for 20 h at 180 °C. The CQD/TiO2 coated plate was rinsed with DI water and annealed at 80 °C overnight.

The coated thin films were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker Alpha Ⅱ), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D2 Phaser), with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM, Joel JSM-IT 800), Raman spectroscopy (Raman microscope, Enspectr) and UV visible spectroscopy (Systronics Type 2202), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 Versa Probe III).

The photocatalytic degradation of Methylene blue (MB) dye by the as prepared catalysts, rutile- TiO2 thin film coated on FTO substrate and CQD/TiO2 thin film coated on FTO substrate were studied under direct sunlight radiation. Each of the thin film catalysts was placed in 200 ml beaker having 100 ml of 3 ppm MB dye solution. The beakers were placed under direct noon sunlight radiation between 11 a.m. and 2 p.m. for degradation studies. For every 30 min, the MB dye solutions were collected and recorded absorption spectrum using UV–visible spectroscopy to evaluate the degradation of MB dye. The percentage of MB dye degradation was estimated by using Eq. (1)
(1)
D y e
d e g r a d a t i o n
( % )
=
(
(
A 0
–
A t
)
/
A 0
)
* 100
where, initial absorbance is denoted as A0 and absorbance at time t is denoted as At.

The phase purity and crystallinity of the grown TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 on the FTO-coated glass plate was evaluated using X-ray diffraction studies. The XRD patterns of the grown thin films were recorded at a scan rate of 2° per minute from 20° to 80° angle is shown Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a, the XRD pattern of TiO2 observed peaks at 27.41°, 36.04°, 41.21°, 54.35° and 63.08° which are corresponding to rutile- TiO2 (JCPDS No. 98-008-0842). The other peaks observed correspond to FTO (JCPDS No. 41–1445) [27]. A peak at 63.08° corresponding to the TiO2 (002) plane is observed relatively high intensity compared to standard data, this could be due to the orientational growth pattern of TiO2 on FTO-coated glass plate [28]. Meng et al. reported comparable tetragonal lattice arrangements of FTO and TiO2 offering growth sites for rutile-phase TiO2 without the help of a rutile seed layer [28]. Fig. 1b, the XRD pattern of CQD/TiO2 reveals a different intensity pattern compared to TiO2 coated on FTO plate (refer Fig. 1a). This confirms the effect of CQDs on TiO2 growth pattern on the FTO plate.

The crystallite dimension of the materials synthesized can be estimated using the Scherrer method, W–H method and the Size-Strain Plot (SSP) procedure. In our study the crystallite dimension was estimated by SSP method [29].

SSP method is mainly used to calculate the isotropic broadening, in general the XRD data provides less intense peaks and may be overlapped. Due to these reasons the strain profile is calculated by the Gaussian function and through Lorentzian function the crystal size will be calculated [30].

Accordingly, we have:
(2)
(
d
h k l
β
h k l
Cos
θ
)
2
=
K λ
D
(
d
h k l
2
β
h k l
Cos
θ
)
+
(
ε 2
)
2
where, the constant K varies with the geometry of the particles.

In the case of sphere-shaped particles, K is taken as 0.75. Fig. 2 a–b shows the SSP plot for pure TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 respectively. The crystallite dimension was calculated using the slope of the best fit linear plot, which is 32.28 nm for TiO2 and 26.98 nm for CQD/TiO2. The concentration of added element has a vital role in the crystallite size of the parent element, higher the concentration, smaller is the crystallite size [31]. Due to the presence of CQDs, the crystallite size of TiO2 reduced from 32.28 nm to 26.98 nm. The transmission of photoexcited electrons to the surface of the photocatalyst is much more efficient in smaller sized nanoparticle; the recombination rate decreases, and therefore the photocatalytic performance enhances [32].

FT-IR was studied for the prepared thin films coated on FTO plate to characterize the functional groups and to predict the molecular structure. Fig. 3 a shows the FT-IR pattern of the rutile- TiO2 ,the observed characteristic peaks at 620 cm−1 and 716 cm−1 which corresponds to Ti–O stretching band, this confirms that the TiO2 phase has been formed [33]. In Fig. 3b, the absorption peaks observed at 2473 cm−1, 1573 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 correspond to Sp2 C–H, CO and C–OH vibrations respectively and the peak at 1039 cm−1 can be assigned to Ti–O–C vibration [34]. The observed bands below 1000 cm−1 could be due to the effect of Ti–O–C and Ti–O–Ti vibrations [34]. The obtained results for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films were comparable with the reported literature [35,36].

Raman spectra were studied to evidence the presence of carbon in the prepared composite thin film. In Fig. 4 a, four distinct bands at 127 cm−1, 218 cm−1, 441 cm−1, 610 cm−1 are observed corresponding to rutile phase TiO2. Assigned Raman modes are as described in the following: 127 cm−1 (O–Ti–O symmetric bending vibration, B1g); 218 cm−1 (multi phonon process, #); 441 cm−1 (O–Ti–O symmetric stretching vibration, Eg); and 610 cm−1 (O–Ti–O anti-symmetric bending vibration, A1g) [36]. These results are well consistent with the XRD analysis (refer section 3.1) to affirm TiO2 is formed. The Raman spectra of CQD/TiO2 coated on FTO substrate (Fig. 4b) observed a peak at 1492 cm−1 which corresponds to D band and a small peak at 1603 cm−1 corresponding to G band [37]. This is evident the CQDs composite with TiO2.

UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 5 ) shows that the absorption peak at 370 nm corresponds to the characteristic peak of CQDs (in solution form). This confirms CQDs formed from sugarcane juice by the hydrothermal method [26].

The energy bandgap of the prepared TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin film was calculated using Kubelka–Munk equation (3):
(3)
(
F
( R )
h ν
)
= A
(
h ν − E g
)
n
where, ν frequency, Eg is energy bandgap, h Planck's constant, A is constant of proportionality, F(R) reflectance coefficient.

The Tauc plot for (F(R)hν)1/2 vs. photon energy (hν) is shown in Fig. 6 a–b. The band gap was calculated by taking the tangent intercept on the plot's X-axis. The energy band gap of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films were estimated to be 3.0 eV and 2.87 eV respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) studies
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films coated on FTO glass plates were analyzed using FESEM studies. Fig. 7 a–b shows the top and cross-sectional view of the grown rutile-TiO2 on the FTO glass plate respectively. The micrographs reveal nanorods of TiO2 in bundles grown on FTO glass substrate. The nanorod-bundles were perpendicular to the FTO substrate of length ∼3.5 μm. The SEM images are well consistent with the XRD analysis (ref. Fig. 1a) to affirm that the orientational growth pattern of TiO2 on the FTO glass plate [37]. Fig. 7c–d shows the top and cross-sectional view of CQD/TiO2 coated on the FTO glass plate which also appears like TiO2. The EDX spectra confirms the presence of Ti, O and C, Ti, O shown in Fig. 8 a–b for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films respectively.

The chemical states of the prepared thin film was investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies. Fig. 9 shows survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS spectral regions of Ti 2p, C 1s, and O 1s of the prepared CQD/TiO2 thin film. The survey spectrum (Fig. 9a) shows that the O, Ti, C elements are present. In Fig. 9a, two strong peaks observed at 459.06 eV and 465.4 eV can be assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 respectively of Ti4+ [38]. The shift in the peak could be due to the strong interaction between CQDs and TiO2 [34]. The shoulder peak observed at 460.6 eV is assigned to Ti3+ [39]. Fig. 9c, shows peaks of O 1s centred at 529.6 eV, 531.2 eV, and 533.07 eV corresponds to CO, Ti–O, and H–O respectively. The H–O peak at 533.07 eV could be due to absorbed H2O on their surface. Fig. 9c, the XPS spectra of C 1s shows the deconvoluted four peaks i,e 284.4 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.5eV which could be attributed to four different chemical valency states of carbon i,e non-oxygenated carbon, carbon in OH bonds, carbon in C–O bonds, and carboxylate carbon (O–CO) respectively [34].

Fig. 10 a & b shows the absorption spectra of MB dye for different time catalytic activity of TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin film catalyst in the direct noon sunlight radiation. The graph of C/C0 versus time (Fig. 10c) shows 78% and 82.6% for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 composite respectively, this affirms the role of CQD to have enhanced dye degradation as electron reservoir [40]. The plots –ln(C/C0) shown in Fig. 9d indicates that the reaction is first order kinetics with rate constants 0.0061 min−1 and 0.0066 min−1 for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 respectively. Estrada Flores et al. reported 82.2% of MB degradation using nanoporous TiO2 thin film photocatalyst under UV radiation [41]. The photocatalytic activity of the GO/TiO2 thin films for MB dye degradation was investigated by Velasco-Hernandez et al. and reported 92% of degradation under UV irradiation [42]. In comparison with these results, our work shows superior photocatalytic activity under the direct sunlight radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism and reusability of photo catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Depending on the estimated valence band maxima and conduction band minima positions for the prepared thin film-CQD/TiO2 photocatalyst, a possible mechanism for photodegradation of dye can be described as follows.

The CQD/TiO2 thin film under direct noon sunlight radiation absorbs energy greater than band gap energy and generates holes in the valence band (VB,
h
V B
+
) and electrons in the conduction band (CB,
e
C B
−
) (equation (4)). CQDs are well-known good electronic conductors, the chemical bond between CQDs and TiO2 can favour photogenerated electron transfer, leading to enhanced carrier separation and reduced recombination rate [17]. The electrons trapped by the CQDs interact with O2 to form superoxide radicals (
∙ O
2 −
), and the holes in the VB oxidize H2O to form hydroxyl radical (OH−). The energy band scheme for CQD/TiO2 thin film is shown in Fig. 11 a.
(4)
T i
O 2
+ h ν
→
e
C B
−
+
h
V B
+

The conduction band edge potential (ECB) and valence band edge potential (EVB) of the TiO2 semiconductor explain the oxidation and reduction process of the photogenerated e− - h+ pair in the photocatalysis process [43]. It is calculated using the following equations,
(5)
E
C B
= χ −
E e
− 0.5
E g
(6)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E g
where, Eg is the band gap of CQD/TiO2 determined by Kubelka Munk function and Tauc plot, Ee the free electron energy which is 4.5 eV, and χ is the absolute electronegativity of TiO2 (5.8 eV) [44]. The calculated ECB and EVB values for the CQD/TiO2 composite thin film are −0.16 eV and +2.76 eV respectively. The ECB of the prepared CQD/TiO2 is more negative than the reduction potential of H2O/H2 (0.05 V vs. NHE, pH = 0), and the EVB is more positive than the oxidation potential of O2/H2O (+2.38 V vs. NHE, pH = 0) to generate reactive hydroxyl radical (OH) and superoxide radicals by accepting hole and electron respectively. The dye degradation was achieved through an oxidation process involving both OH− radicals and
∙ O
2 −
radicals.

The reusability of the CQD/TiO2 thin film was examined for three cycles and the outcomes are shown in Fig. 11b. It shows 83%, 80% and 80% percentage of MB degradation for the first, second and the third cycles respectively. This concludes that CQD/TiO2 thin film catalyst is quite stable and recoverable. Hence, this CQD/TiO2 thin film can be a potential candidate for water treatment.

TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 thin films were grown on FTO glass substrate by an energy efficient, cost effective and simple one-step hydrothermal technique. The XRD studies provided phase and orientational information of the grown samples. The crystallite size of the grown samples was calculated and analyzed using SSP model. The FT-IR, FESEM, and EDX analysis qualitatively confirmed the presence of CQDs on CQD/TiO2 thin film. The band gap estimated by UV-DRS for TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 was found to be 3.0 eV and 2.87 eV respectively. The CQDs inclusion acted as an electron reservoir which enabled better charge separation and reduced recombination rate to have enhanced MB dye degradation. As a result, CQD/TiO2 thin film is a promising material for better photocatalytic performance and also has broad range of environmental applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support granted via the University seed grant (File No: RI&C/Funded Project/SI-3 & 4 dated 08/11/2021) and thank the management of Presidency University, Bangalore, India. The authors would also like to thank Saveetha Dental College, White Lab, Chennai, for providing characterization facilities of FESEM, EDX, FTIR, SIT Tumukur for XPS and Bruker Center of Excellence, Bangalore for XRD.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light irradiation by non-peripherally tetra substituted phthalocyanine-TiO2 nanocomposites
Inorg. Chim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Gorduk, O. Avciata, and U. Avciata, “Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light irradiation by non-peripherally tetra substituted phthalocyanine-TiO2 nanocomposites,” Inorganica Chim. Acta, vol. 471, pp. 137-147, 2018, doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ica.2017.11.004.

J. Alloys Compd.

Alloys Compd., vol. 869, pp. 159389, 2021, doi: 10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.159389.
